title: Dog Non-Weight Bearing on Right Hind Leg: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do if your dog isn't putting weight on their back right leg. Discover potential causes, urgency levels, and how to help your vet.
content_html:
What Is Non-Weight Bearing Lameness?

When a dog is non-weight bearing on their right hind leg, it means they are holding the limb up entirely and refusing to touch it to the ground while standing or walking. This behavior is typically a sign of significant pain, discomfort, or a mechanical inability to use the limb, often involving the bones, joints, or ligaments.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a non-weight bearing limb is categorized as Medium. While this is rarely immediately life-threatening, it indicates a serious injury that requires veterinary attention within 12 to 24 hours. You should seek immediate emergency care if you notice protruding bone, heavy bleeding, extreme lethargy, or if your dog is unable to move their other limbs.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo or a short video of your dog can be a vital tool for a triage professional. A photo of the leg from the side or back can help identify visible swelling, bruising, or an abnormal angle (deformity). A video of your dog attempting to walk helps the vet observe their gait and determine which joint is most affected, ensuring your pet receives the correct priority level for an appointment.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin or ibuprofen for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Human pain medications can be extremely toxic to dogs and may cause stomach ulcers or kidney failure. Never administer medication without a veterinarian's direct advice.

faqs.question: Should I try to splint the leg myself?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid splinting or wrapping the leg at home. Improperly applied bandages can cut off circulation or cause further damage to a potential fracture.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of back leg lameness?
faqs.answer: Common causes include a Cranial Cruciate Ligament (CCL) tear, a luxating patella (dislocated kneecap), hip dysplasia flare-ups, or injuries to the paw pad such as a thorn or a torn nail.
